impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's conditioning of patent licenses on exclusive purchase of unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act, and whether the defense that tied products are 'nonstaple goods' lacking substantial non-infringing uses shields the patent holder from antitrust liability.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding that Ingevity engaged in unlawful tying by conditioning licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers' agreements to exclusively purchase Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld the antitrust liability and corresponding damages award, rejecting Ingevity's argument that its honeycombs were nonstaple goods protected from antitrust scrutiny under patent law.
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors of patent holders who engage in tying arrangements can obtain damages under antitrust law, even where the tied products are related to the patented technology.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face significant antitrust liability risk if they condition patent licenses on exclusive purchases of unpatented products, even when the tied products are closely related to the patented invention. Companies with strong patent portfolios must carefully structure licensing to avoid tying claims.

impacts.caseName: Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to methods for detecting anomalous program executions by comparing function calls made in an emulator to a model of function calls are abstract and ineligible for patenting under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated a $185 million infringement judgment against Norton (Gen Digital), holding that Columbia's asserted cybersecurity patent claims were abstract at Alice step one and remanding for step two analysis. The court also reversed the district court's denial of JMOL on damages from foreign sales and vacated enhanced damages and attorneys' fees awards that relied on an improper contempt finding against Norton's counsel.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies accused of infringing software patents gain a stronger basis for challenging patent eligibility of claims directed to computer security methods, potentially reducing exposure to large damages verdicts.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the cybersecurity and software space face heightened risk that their claims will be found patent-ineligible under § 101, potentially undermining significant patent portfolios and licensing revenue streams.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to a server that tailors content delivery specifications to a wireless device's screen size and capabilities are ineligible for patenting under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held on Netflix's cross-appeal that GoTV's streaming media patent claims were ineligible under § 101, reversing the district court's judgment and entering judgment for Netflix. The court found the claims were directed to the abstract idea of tailoring content to device capabilities, requiring reversal of a $2.5 million jury verdict in GoTV's favor.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming and technology companies gain additional precedent to defeat patent infringement claims based on content delivery patents as abstract under § 101.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the content delivery and streaming technology space face increased vulnerability to § 101 challenges, reducing the enforceability of patents covering adaptive content delivery methods.

impacts.caseName: US Patent No. 7,679,637 LLC v. Google LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to web conferencing systems with time-shifting capabilities for asynchronous viewing of data streams are ineligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed dismissal of a patent infringement suit against Google, holding that claims relating to web conferencing with time-shifting capabilities failed to state a claim. The decision reinforces the trend of invalidating software method patents under § 101 at the pleading stage.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies can defeat patent infringement suits at the motion to dismiss stage based on § 101 ineligibility, significantly reducing litigation costs.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent assertion entities and patent holders with web-based technology claims face the risk of early dismissal, diminishing the litigation value of such patents.

impacts.caseName: Ascendis Pharma A/S v. BioMarin Pharmaceutical Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether an ITC respondent that missed the deadline for seeking a mandatory stay under 28 U.S.C. § 1659(a)(2) of its original declaratory judgment action may obtain a mandatory stay by voluntarily dismissing that action and refiling a new one.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that an ITC respondent may not circumvent the statutory deadline for a mandatory stay of a parallel district court action by dismissing and refiling the action. BioMarin missed the 30-day deadline to request a stay of Ascendis's original declaratory judgment action and attempted to obtain one after Ascendis refiled; the court held that this gamesmanship was impermissible under § 1659(a)(2).
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent challengers (declaratory judgment plaintiffs) are protected from strategic manipulation of mandatory stay provisions by ITC respondents, preserving their ability to proceed with district court actions in parallel with ITC investigations.
impacts.negativeImplications: ITC respondents who miss the 30-day deadline for a mandatory stay cannot cure the deficiency through procedural maneuvering, limiting their ability to consolidate disputes at the ITC and potentially forcing parallel litigation in district court.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether genuine disputes of material fact precluded summary judgment on inequitable conduct in obtaining patents and on a Walker Process fraud claim for attempted monopolization based on enforcement of patents obtained through fraud.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgments for both parties, holding that genuine disputes of material fact existed regarding whether Tenaris committed inequitable conduct in obtaining its coiled tubing patents by failing to disclose prior CYMAX documents, and whether Global Tubing could establish Walker Process fraud for attempted monopolization. The case was remanded for trial on both issues.
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors who discover undisclosed prior art during litigation can pursue both inequitable conduct defenses and Walker Process antitrust counterclaims, creating significant leverage against patent holders who may have withheld material information during prosecution.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face the risk that failure to disclose known prior art during prosecution may expose them to both patent unenforceability and antitrust liability, with both questions going to trial rather than being resolved on summary judgment.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court erred in excluding a patent plaintiff's damages expert report and granting summary judgment for absence of a remedy based on that exclusion.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report and vacated summary judgment in Microsoft's favor, holding that the lower court improperly excluded the expert's opinions on reasonable royalty damages for patent infringement relating to virtual network optimization technology. The case was remanded for further proceedings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent plaintiffs benefit from a more permissive standard for admission of damages expert testimony, reducing the risk that Daubert challenges will be used to effectively eliminate their cases before trial.
impacts.negativeImplications: Accused infringers face reduced ability to obtain summary judgment by challenging damages experts under Daubert, potentially increasing the costs and risks of patent infringement litigation.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC correctly found that Apple's importation and sale of Apple Watch models incorporating blood oxygen measurement functionality violated § 337 of the Tariff Act by infringing Masimo's patents.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the ITC's finding that Apple violated § 337 by importing and selling Apple Watch models that infringed Masimo's wearable blood oxygen measurement patents. The court upheld the limited exclusion order barring importation of infringing Apple Watch models, finding no error in the Commission's domestic industry, validity, or infringement determinations.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders with strong IP in medical device and wearable technology can effectively enforce patents through the ITC's exclusion order mechanism against major technology companies importing infringing products.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies incorporating health monitoring features in consumer devices face significant import exclusion risk at the ITC, potentially requiring product redesigns or licensing agreements to continue selling affected products in the U.S.

impacts.caseName: 112 Genesee Street, LLC v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Restaurant Revitalization Fund statute created a money-mandating obligation enforceable under the Tucker Act when eligible restaurants applied in the required order but were not awarded grants before funds were exhausted.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the Court of Federal Claims' denial of the government's motion to dismiss, holding that over 300 restaurants had Tucker Act jurisdiction to pursue claims for unpaid Restaurant Revitalization Fund grants. The court found that the statutory language directing the SBA to 'award grants' in the order applications were received created a mandatory obligation, giving applicants who applied before others who received grants an enforceable claim for damages.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses eligible for mandatory federal grant programs gain a viable cause of action when agencies fail to follow statutory disbursement requirements, establishing important precedent for government payment obligations.
impacts.negativeImplications: The federal government faces potential liability for grant programs where statutory language mandates disbursement in a specified order but administrative processes resulted in out-of-order payments.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly approved a $125 million class action settlement resolving claims that the federal judiciary charged excessive PACER fees beyond those authorized by statute.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed approval of a $125 million nationwide class action settlement resolving claims that the federal judiciary exceeded its statutory authority by using PACER fees to fund expenses unrelated to PACER operations. The court upheld the settlement terms, attorneys' fees, and incentive awards to class representative nonprofit organizations, rejecting objections regarding jurisdiction, adequacy, and propriety of service awards.
impacts.positiveImplications: PACER users, including law firms and businesses that regularly access court records, will receive substantial reimbursement for excessive fees paid during the eight-year class period.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the International Trade Commission's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential in countervailing duty and antidumping investigations is consistent with the governing statute, and whether such designation abrogates the common law right of public access to judicial records.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the Court of International Trade's holding that the ITC's blanket confidentiality practice for questionnaire responses in trade investigations is incompatible with the statute and does not override the common law right of access to judicial records. The court held that 19 U.S.C. § 1516a does not abrogate public access rights and that confidentiality must be determined on a case-by-case basis consistent with statutory criteria.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies and trade associations gain greater access to information submitted in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations, improving transparency in trade proceedings and enabling more effective participation.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies that submit sensitive business information in response to ITC questionnaires face reduced assurance of confidentiality, potentially exposing proprietary data such as pricing, costs, and supply chain details to competitors and the public.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the Patent Trial and Appeal Board governing discretionary denial of inter partes review institution constitute general statements of policy exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking requirements.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's Fintiv-related instructions governing discretionary denial of IPR institution are general statements of policy exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking under 5 U.S.C. § 553(b). The court emphasized that Congress provided no legal right to IPR institution, the instructions do not bind the Director, and non-institution decisions are statutorily insulated from judicial review.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the PTO's broad discretion to deny IPR institution based on parallel litigation and other factors, preserving more flexibility to enforce patents without facing administrative review challenges.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies and other frequent IPR petitioners face continued risk of discretionary denial of their IPR petitions under the Director's policy guidance, without the procedural protections of notice-and-comment rulemaking.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether means-plus-function claim terms in a crop harvester patent were indefinite for failing to disclose sufficient corresponding structure in the specification.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's finding that asserted claims of a crop harvester height control patent were invalid as indefinite. The court held that the specification adequately disclosed the corresponding structure for the 'control means' limitation, rejecting Deere's argument that conventional controller components described in the specification were insufficiently detailed.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders with means-plus-function claims benefit from a more forgiving standard for specification disclosure of corresponding structure, particularly where the specification describes conventional components understood by skilled artisans.
impacts.negativeImplications: Accused infringers face reduced ability to invalidate means-plus-function claims as indefinite when the specification references conventional structures, even if described at a high level.

impacts.caseName: Willis Electric Co., Ltd. v. Polygroup Ltd. (Macao Commercial Offshore)
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly denied JMOL of obviousness and a new trial on damages for a pre-lit artificial tree patent with coaxial trunk connectors.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed denial of Polygroup's motions for JMOL of obviousness and a new trial on damages for Willis's patent covering pre-lit artificial trees with integrated mechanical-electrical trunk connections. The court upheld the jury's findings on non-obviousness and the damages award, sustaining patent protection for innovative consumer product designs.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in consumer products gain reinforcement that combination patents with demonstrable commercial success and market praise can withstand obviousness challenges at the JMOL stage.
impacts.negativeImplications: Competitors in the consumer products space face continued exposure to patent enforcement where patents cover product integration innovations, even where individual elements may have existed in prior art.
